title: The Falling-Asleep of the Virgin
subtitle: Mary's last years on Mount Sion, her dormition, and her bodily Assumption

stillness: Sit with her in the silence after the Ascension.
gospel: and with Mary, the Mother of Jesus."
Of her death, of her Assumption,
What is left, then, but to seek by reason
that which is fitting to the truth?
behold: The Blessed Virgin remained, as long as she lived, on the mountain of Sion,
in the cell where, after the Ascension of her Son, she gave herself to prayer,
turning over in her heart all that she had come to know.
Nearby was the cell of blessed John the Evangelist —
the first church of the world,
where he offered the first Mass in the sight of the Blessed Virgin.
Consider with what fires of grief and of love she burned in that time.
She would turn over in her mind all that she had heard, all she had seen, all she had known;
and often she was refreshed by visiting the places —
the footprints of his Ascension, the place of his burial and Resurrection,
every place in which he had suffered, she went to look upon;
not that she sought the living among the dead,
but that she might console herself with the sight of them.
And the One she had borne, she embraced in the arms of her mind, with faithful spirit.
Then, in the cell beside her own, she passed over to heaven,
taken up by the angels.
All the Apostles are said to have been present at her falling-asleep,
and to have buried her in the valley of Josaphat,
where her most venerable tomb is shown —
held in the highest reverence even among the Saracens —
yet now is found empty to those who gaze upon it.
consider: By what order the Blessed Virgin passed from here to the realms above, no catholic history tells.
Not even John the Evangelist, who took her as his own beneath the Cross, is read to have left anything of this in writing.
No one could have told it more faithfully, had God willed to make it known, than he who took her up to nourish.
It remains, then, that a man not falsely feign as open what God willed to remain hidden.
Many of us are in doubt whether she was taken up together with her body, or died with her body left behind.
How, or at what time, or by what persons her most holy body was borne away from there, or where it was carried, or whether it rose again — this is not known.
Which of these is the truer, we are uncertain;
yet we commit it rather to God, to whom nothing is impossible, than rashly to define by our own authority what we cannot prove.
Mindful of the human condition, we do not fear to call that death of the Most Blessed a death in time.
But if we say she was dissolved into the common rottenness, the worm, and ashes — we must weigh whether this befits so great a sanctity, so great a prerogative of God's hall.
To the first parent it was said: dust you are, and into dust you shall return;
the flesh of Christ escaped this condition, which bore no corruption — and from that flesh the Virgin's nature was taken.
To the woman God said: I will multiply your sorrows; in pain you shall bring forth.
Sorrow holy Mary bore, whose soul the sword of Christ's Passion pierced through;
yet without pain Mary gave birth — therefore she is excepted from things common, she whom so great a dignity exalts.
If God willed to keep his Mother whole in the modesty of virginity, why would he not will to keep her uncorrupted from the stench of decay?
Does it not belong to the goodness of the Lord to guard his Mother's honor —
He who came not to loose the Law, but to fulfill it?
He who in life honored her above all others by the grace of his conception —
it is a holy thing to believe that in death he honored her with a singular salvation, and a special grace.
prayer: I beg you — if there is in you any heart of compassion —
consider with what love she was tormented,
with what longing this Virgin burned,
while she turned over in her soul all that she had heard,
all that she had seen, all that she had known.
I think that whatever there is of heart, whatever of mind, whatever of human strength,
if you bring it all to bear, it will not suffice
to grasp how unceasingly she was tormented with the fire of holiest love,
how she was moved by desires, filled with the Holy Spirit,
how she was carried away by the wonder of heavenly secrets.

latinJewel.latin: Et ideo, quam saepe locorum recreabatur visitationibus, et quem genuerat fideli spiritu, mentis complexabatur amplexibus!
latinJewel.english: And so, how often was she refreshed by visiting those places, and embraced in the arms of her mind, with faithful spirit, the One she had borne!
